mappings.rationaleAI: Antarctica covers scientific exploration of one of Earth's most extreme environments, including ice sheet research (A.1.a), and its history as a proving ground for exploration technology and life-support systems (D.1).

Exoplanets covers detection methods (radial velocity, transit photometry, direct imaging) and space missions discovering planets orbiting other stars — directly relevant to the use of unmanned spacecraft to probe planets and their satellites (C.2.b.iii).

Covers the planets of our solar system, their characteristics, and exoplanet discoveries through space missions like Kepler — relevant to unmanned probes of planets (C.2.b.iii) and contributions of space exploration to physical sciences (C.4).

Mars covers robotic exploration missions (Viking, Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity), the search for habitability, and future human exploration — directly relevant to unmanned spacecraft probing planets (C.2.b.iii) and manned space programs (C.2.c).

Saturn covers the Cassini-Huygens mission's discoveries about the planet, its rings, and moons including Titan and Enceladus — directly relevant to unmanned spacecraft probing planets and satellites (C.2.b.iii).

Covers the scientific search for extraterrestrial life including SETI, the Drake equation, conditions for habitability, and astrobiology — relevant to the use of unmanned spacecraft and satellites in scientific exploration (C.2.b) and contributions to physical sciences (C.4).

Covers the history of oceanography from HMS Challenger, ocean floor mapping, deep-sea research, hydrothermal vents, and marine exploration technology — directly relevant to undersea exploration platforms (B.1), navigational methods (B.2), and sampling techniques (B.3).

The Challenger Expedition pioneered oceanographic methods using HMS Challenger as a surface vessel (B.1.a), discovering thousands of species and mapping ocean floors using early measurement techniques (B.3).
Seismology covers how seismic waves reveal Earth's internal structure including core, mantle, and crust — directly relevant to indirect geophysical methods of exploration (A.2.a).
Vulcanology covers volcanic formation, eruption mechanisms, and monitoring/prediction efforts — relevant to scientific exploration determining the properties of Earth's interior (A.1.a).

Ageing the Earth covers radiometric dating, stratigraphic principles, and isotope geochemistry used to determine Earth's age — directly relevant to geophysical and geochemical methods of exploration (A.2.a) and scientific determination of Earth's properties (A.1.a).

Early geology covers the emergence of geological science including stratigraphic mapping, uniformitarianism, and methods of dating and understanding geological strata — relevant to the scientific exploration of Earth's properties (A.1.a) and exploration methods (A.2).

Asteroids covers their discovery, classification, near-Earth impact hazards, and what they reveal about solar system formation — relevant to space probes of solar system bodies (C.2.b.iii) and contributions to physical sciences (C.4).
Comets covers their composition, orbital mechanics, and modern space missions including Giotto and Rosetta — directly relevant to unmanned spacecraft probing solar system bodies (C.2.b.iii).
